FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 5.1 data release, 21 recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10; methods confirm NIH-funded longitudinal cohort.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical CPM analysis applying connectome-based predictive modelling to resting-state fMRI and SMAQ scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) is a qualifying problematic-SM scale (listed in Step 1 phone/SM-specific variables). It is paired with whole-brain resting-state functional connectivity (neural individual-trait). CPM predicts SMAQ scores from connectivity matrices; combined positive and negative networks: r = 0.055, p = 0.016.

FT_IC3.evidence: Pearson r = 0.055 (p = 0.016) and r = 0.066 (p = 0.003) for observed vs predicted SMAQ; permutation p = 0.02; positive network r, negative network r all reported numerically.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates: r values, permutation p-values, edge counts (1212 positive, 1295 negative), all quantifying the brain-SMAQ relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions hold: (a) longitudinal design — baseline fMRI (ages 9-10) predicts SMAQ at 4-year follow-up; (b) SMAQ/SM is the DV in every analysis — CPM predicts SMAQ severity from brain connectivity; (c) no analysis anywhere uses SMAQ or SM as IV predicting an outcome. No cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis exists to trigger the carve-out. Paper is excluded under FT-EC7.
notes: CPM predicts SMAQ (problematic SM use) from resting-state fMRI baseline. Neural connectivity is the predictor (IV); SMAQ is always the outcome (DV). No SM-as-IV analysis anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 5.1 data release, 21 sites, two-year follow-up data; methods confirm NIH-funded longitudinal cohort of children aged 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical CPM analysis applying connectome-based predictive modelling to task-based fMRI and VGAQ scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The sole behavioral variable is VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), measuring problematic gaming severity. Platform is unspecified — participants self-reported engagement with 'single-player or online multi-player video games' with no explicit mobile/phone-based gaming modality. VGAQ is a generic gaming variable (binding clarification: VGAQ fails IC2 unless explicitly mobile/phone-based or paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable). No phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient appears anywhere in the paper (main results, supplementary Tables S1-S6, generalizability analyses). Step 2 not applicable (no composite screen time). FT-IC2 = NOT_MET under the gaming carve-out.

notes: Paper studies VGAQ (problematic gaming severity) as a DV predicted from fMRI connectivity. Gaming platform unspecified; no phone/SM-specific variable with own coefficient anywhere in the paper. Excluded at FT-IC2 under gaming carve-out.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 5.0 data release, 21 sites, children aged 9-10; methods confirm NIH-funded cohort (N ~ 10,000).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed-effects models and mediation analysis on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Supplementary Table S1 (referenced in paper: 'We also examined the relationship between screen time on each digital device and each domain of cognitive function, and summarized the results in supporting information (Table S1 and Fig. S1)') reports per-device breakouts including texting and video chat — qualifying phone/SM-specific variables — each paired with cognitive function domains (executive function, processing speed, working memory, episodic memory, language), which are individual-trait-level outcomes. Regression coefficients (b and beta) with 95% CIs and FDR-corrected p-values are reported for each device type vs each cognitive domain. Step 1 satisfied: texting and video chat are listed SM/phone-specific variables with their own numeric estimates paired with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression b and beta coefficients with 95% CIs and FDR p-values reported throughout (e.g., executive function: b = -0.212, beta = -0.051, FDR p < .001); mediation indirect effects with bootstrap 95% CIs also reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: beta coefficients, b coefficients, 95% CIs, FDR-adjusted p-values, and bootstrap indirect effects for all screen time measures vs all cognitive domains.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (authors explicitly note 'cross-sectional design' as a limitation). EC7 requires longitudinal design; this is cross-sectional. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Primary analysis uses a composite screen time variable (TV + videos + video games + texting + video chat) that bundles non-phone items; however Supplementary Table S1 breaks out per-device estimates including texting and video chat as separate phone/SM-specific predictors of cognitive outcomes, satisfying IC2 Step 1. Cross-sectional; screen time is IV, cognitive function is DV.
